title: Lieb-Schultz-Mattis theorem from gauge constraints
abstract: We construct a $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_2$ gauge theory coupled to matter on a one-dimensional chain, aiming to study the ground-state physics in the Gauss law subspace. We show that the theory in the Gauss law subspace has a U$(1)$ symmetry whose generator commutes with lattice translations, but anticommutes with the lattice reflection operator. This leads to a Lieb-Schultz-Mattis (LSM) theorem that always rules out a trivial gapped ground state in the Gauss law subspace, if the hamiltonian is invariant under translations and reflection. Any point in the parameter space must realize a either a spontaneously symmetry broken (SSB) ground state, or a gapless ground state. Imposing the Gauss law is pivotal for the existence of the U$(1)$ symmetry, and hence of the LSM theorem. We thus demonstrate a novel mechanism to obtain an LSM-type theorem, wherein the symmetry responsible for the theorem originates from the kinematic constraints of a gauge theory. We identify a point in the parameter space at which the system is gapless. At the gapless point, the excitations admit a description in terms of free Dirac fermions with a constraint on the total fermion number. The asymptotic behavior of the two-point correlation function of the simplest local gauge-invariant quantity at the gapless point is found to be $ \propto \cos{(πr)}\,r^{-2/9}$, where $r$ is the lattice separation between the two points. This model is also a natural platform to study phase diagram topological defects residing in families of SSB phases.
summary: The paper shows that the kinematic constraints of a gauge theory can act as a source for a Lieb-Schultz-Mattis theorem, preventing a trivial gapped ground state. By studying a Z2 x Z2 gauge theory, the authors identify a critical point described by free Dirac fermions with a central charge of c=1. This demonstrates that gauge-induced symmetries can fundamentally dictate the phase diagram of a quantum system.
main_problem: Investigating whether the kinematic constraints of a gauge theory (Gauss law) can generate a Lieb-Schultz-Mattis (LSM) type theorem.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the Gauss law in a Z2 x Z2 gauge theory induces a U(1) symmetry that anticommutes with lattice reflection, thereby ruling out a trivial gapped ground state and forcing either gaplessness or spontaneous symmetry breaking.
method: The study employs algebraic mapping of the gauge-constrained Hilbert space, Jordan-Wigner transformation to a fermionic representation, Toeplitz determinant analysis for correlations, and DMRG for numerical verification of the central charge.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional periodic chain of spin-1 operators subject to a Z2 x Z2 gauge theory with a local Gauss law constraint.
key_observables: Ground state energy, bipartite entanglement entropy, central charge (c=1), and two-point correlation functions of gauge-invariant quantities.
important_parameters: Hopping amplitudes (t_alpha), potential strengths (K_alpha), and the thermodynamic limit (L -> infinity).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes periodic boundary conditions and relies on the validity of the Fisher-Hartwig conjecture for asymptotic correlation decay.
figures_summary: A plot of entanglement entropy versus subsystem size used to extract the central charge.
paper_structure: The paper begins by constructing the gauge theory and Gauss law subspace, moves to deriving the effective Hamiltonian and its symmetries, proves the LSM theorem via variational methods, analyzes the critical point using fermion mapping and Toeplitz determinants, and concludes with numerical verification via DMRG.
why_interesting: This work provides a novel mechanism for generating topological constraints (LSM theorems) from gauge-induced symmetries, which is highly relevant to the study of constrained dynamics and many-body physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) - The one-dimensional chain on which the model is defined. Sites are shown in cyan, while the links are depicted in magenta. As per the convention used, the link jτ connects the sites jσ and j + 1σ. The hopping terms hα j are defined on the links and the gauge transformations Aα j′ are defined on the sites. (b) - In the Gauss law subspace, for any given site, the states on the site and the two links touching it must be all different. For example, the configuration shown on the left is valid, but the one on the right is not allowed as the states on the two links are the same.
